title: Graph quadratics in factored form
description: Graph quadratic functions that are given in factored form. For example, graph y=(x-4)(x+2).
topic_path: Get ready for transformations of functions and modeling with functions

hints:
##Finding the zeros

$\begin{align}
\dfrac18(x-6)&(x+2)=0
\\\\
\swarrow\quad&\quad\searrow
\\\\
x-6=0\quad&\quad x+2=0
\\\\
x=\goldE{6}\quad&\quad x=\goldE{-2}
\end{align}$

##Finding the vertex

The vertex's $x$-coordinate is always exactly between the two zeros:

$\text{vertex's }x\text{-coordinate}=\dfrac{\goldE{6}+(\goldE{-2})}{2}=\blueE{2}$

To find the vertex's $y$-coordinate, we can plug $x=\blueE{2}$ in the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\dfrac18(\blueE{2}-6)(\blueE{2}+2)
\\\\
&=\dfrac18(-4)(4)
\\\\
&=-2
\end{align}$

##Graphing

So the vertex is at $(2,-2)$.

In order to graph, we need the vertex and another point. That other point can be one of the zeros we found, like $(6,0)$:

[[☃ grapher 1]]

##Finding the zeros

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{5}{16}(x -1)&(x -9)=0
\\\\
\swarrow\quad&\quad\searrow
\\\\
x -1=0\quad&\quad x -9=0
\\\\
x=\goldD{1}\quad&\quad x=\goldD{9}
\end{align}$

##Finding the vertex

The vertex's $x$-coordinate is always exactly between the two zeros:

$\text{vertex's }x\text{-coordinate}=\dfrac{\goldD{1}+\goldD{9}}{2}=\blueD{5}$

To find the vertex's $y$-coordinate, we can plug $x=\blueD{5}$ in the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\dfrac{5}{16}(\blueD{5} -1)(\blueD{5} -9)
\\\\
&=\dfrac{5}{16}(4)(-4)
\\\\
&=-5
\end{align}$

##Graphing

So the vertex is at $(5,-5)$.

In order to graph, we need the vertex and another point. That other point can be one of the zeros we found, like $(1,0)$:

[[☃ grapher 1]]

##Finding the zeros

$\begin{align}
4(x +6)&(x +4)=0
\\\\
\swarrow\quad&\quad\searrow
\\\\
x +6=0\quad&\quad x +4=0
\\\\
x=\goldD{-6}\quad&\quad x=\goldD{-4}
\end{align}$

##Finding the vertex

The vertex's $x$-coordinate is always exactly between the two zeros:

$\text{vertex's }x\text{-coordinate}=\dfrac{\goldD{-6}+(\goldD{-4})}{2}=\blueD{-5}$

To find the vertex's $y$-coordinate, we can plug $x=\blueD{-5}$ in the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}4(\blueD{-5} +6)(\blueD{-5} +4)
\\\\
&=4(1)(-1)
\\\\
&=-4
\end{align}$

##Graphing

So the vertex is at $(-5,-4)$.

In order to graph, we need the vertex and another point. That other point can be one of the zeros we found, like $(-6,0)$:

[[☃ grapher 1]]

##Finding the zeros

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{2}{3}(x +7)&(x +1)=0
\\\\
\swarrow\quad&\quad\searrow
\\\\
x +7=0\quad&\quad x +1=0
\\\\
x=\goldD{-7}\quad&\quad x=\goldD{-1}
\end{align}$

##Finding the vertex

The vertex's $x$-coordinate is always exactly between the two zeros:

$\text{vertex's }x\text{-coordinate}=\dfrac{\goldD{-7}+(\goldD{-1})}{2}=\blueD{-4}$

To find the vertex's $y$-coordinate, we can plug $x=\blueD{-4}$ in the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\dfrac{2}{3}(\blueD{-4} +7)(\blueD{-4} +1)
\\\\
&=\dfrac{2}{3}(3)(-3)
\\\\
&=-6
\end{align}$

##Graphing

So the vertex is at $(-4,-6)$.

In order to graph, we need the vertex and another point. That other point can be one of the zeros we found, like $(-7,0)$:

[[☃ grapher 1]]

##Finding the zeros

$\begin{align}
-\dfrac{3}{4}(x +3)&(x +7)=0
\\\\
\swarrow\quad&\quad\searrow
\\\\
x +3=0\quad&\quad x +7=0
\\\\
x=\goldD{-3}\quad&\quad x=\goldD{-7}
\end{align}$

##Finding the vertex

The vertex's $x$-coordinate is always exactly between the two zeros:

$\text{vertex's }x\text{-coordinate}=\dfrac{\goldD{-3}+(\goldD{-7})}{2}=\blueD{-5}$

To find the vertex's $y$-coordinate, we can plug $x=\blueD{-5}$ in the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}-\dfrac{3}{4}(\blueD{-5} +3)(\blueD{-5} +7)
\\\\
&=-\dfrac{3}{4}(-2)(2)
\\\\
&=3
\end{align}$

##Graphing

So the vertex is at $(-5,3)$.

In order to graph, we need the vertex and another point. That other point can be one of the zeros we found, like $(-3,0)$:

[[☃ grapher 1]]

##Finding the zeros

$\begin{align}
-\dfrac{1}{4}(x -2)&(x +6)=0
\\\\
\swarrow\quad&\quad\searrow
\\\\
x -2=0\quad&\quad x +6=0
\\\\
x=\goldD{2}\quad&\quad x=\goldD{-6}
\end{align}$

##Finding the vertex

The vertex's $x$-coordinate is always exactly between the two zeros:

$\text{vertex's }x\text{-coordinate}=\dfrac{\goldD{2}+(\goldD{-6})}{2}=\blueD{-2}$

To find the vertex's $y$-coordinate, we can plug $x=\blueD{-2}$ in the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}-\dfrac{1}{4}(\blueD{-2} -2)(\blueD{-2} +6)
\\\\
&=-\dfrac{1}{4}(-4)(4)
\\\\
&=4
\end{align}$

##Graphing

So the vertex is at $(-2,4)$.

In order to graph, we need the vertex and another point. That other point can be one of the zeros we found, like $(2,0)$:

[[☃ grapher 1]]

##Finding the zeros

$\begin{align}
-\dfrac{1}{7}(x -7)&(x +7)=0
\\\\
\swarrow\quad&\quad\searrow
\\\\
x -7=0\quad&\quad x +7=0
\\\\
x=\goldD{7}\quad&\quad x=\goldD{-7}
\end{align}$

##Finding the vertex

The vertex's $x$-coordinate is always exactly between the two zeros:

$\text{vertex's }x\text{-coordinate}=\dfrac{\goldD{7}+(\goldD{-7})}{2}=\blueD{0}$

To find the vertex's $y$-coordinate, we can plug $x=\blueD{0}$ in the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}-\dfrac{1}{7}(\blueD{0} -7)(\blueD{0} +7)
\\\\
&=-\dfrac{1}{7}(-7)(7)
\\\\
&=7
\end{align}$

##Graphing

So the vertex is at $(0,7)$.

In order to graph, we need the vertex and another point. That other point can be one of the zeros we found, like $(7,0)$:

[[☃ grapher 1]]

##Finding the zeros

$\begin{align}
-\dfrac{3}{8}(x +5)&(x -3)=0
\\\\
\swarrow\quad&\quad\searrow
\\\\
x +5=0\quad&\quad x -3=0
\\\\
x=\goldD{-5}\quad&\quad x=\goldD{3}
\end{align}$

##Finding the vertex

The vertex's $x$-coordinate is always exactly between the two zeros:

$\text{vertex's }x\text{-coordinate}=\dfrac{\goldD{-5}+\goldD{3}}{2}=\blueD{-1}$

To find the vertex's $y$-coordinate, we can plug $x=\blueD{-1}$ in the expression:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}-\dfrac{3}{8}(\blueD{-1} +5)(\blueD{-1} -3)
\\\\
&=-\dfrac{3}{8}(4)(-4)
\\\\
&=6
\end{align}$

##Graphing

So the vertex is at $(-1,6)$.

In order to graph, we need the vertex and another point. That other point can be one of the zeros we found, like $(-5,0)$:

[[☃ grapher 1]]

##Finding the zeros

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{3}{8}(x -1)&(x -9)=0
\\\\
\swarrow\quad&\quad\searrow
\\\\
x -1=0\quad&\quad x -9=0
\\\\
x=\goldD{1}\quad&\quad x=\goldD{9}
\end{align}$

##Finding the vertex

The vertex's $x$-coordinate is always exactly between the two zeros:

$\text{vertex's }x\text{-coordinate}=\dfrac{\goldD{1}+\goldD{9}}{2}=\blueD{5}$

To find the vertex's $y$-coordinate, we can plug $x=\blueD{5}$ into $f$:

$\begin{align}
f(5)&=\dfrac{3}{8}(\blueD{5} -1)(\blueD{5} -9)
\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{8}(4)(-4)
\\\\
&=-6
\end{align}$

##Graphing

So the vertex is at $(5,-6)$.

In order to graph, we need the vertex and another point. That other point can be one of the zeros we found, like $(1,0)$:

[[☃ grapher 2]]

##Finding the zeros

$\begin{align}
2(x +2)&(x +6)=0
\\\\
\swarrow\quad&\quad\searrow
\\\\
x +2=0\quad&\quad x +6=0
\\\\
x=\goldD{-2}\quad&\quad x=\goldD{-6}
\end{align}$

##Finding the vertex

The vertex's $x$-coordinate is always exactly between the two zeros:

$\text{vertex's }x\text{-coordinate}=\dfrac{\goldD{-2}+(\goldD{-6})}{2}=\blueD{-4}$

To find the vertex's $y$-coordinate, we can plug $x=\blueD{-4}$ into $g$:

$\begin{align}
g(-4)&=2(\blueD{-4} +2)(\blueD{-4} +6)
\\\\
&=2(-2)(2)
\\\\
&=-8
\end{align}$

##Graphing

So the vertex is at $(-4,-8)$.

In order to graph, we need the vertex and another point. That other point can be one of the zeros we found, like $(-2,0)$:

[[☃ grapher 2]]

##Finding the zeros

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{1}{4}(x -1)&(x +7)=0
\\\\
\swarrow\quad&\quad\searrow
\\\\
x -1=0\quad&\quad x +7=0
\\\\
x=\goldD{1}\quad&\quad x=\goldD{-7}
\end{align}$

##Finding the vertex

The vertex's $x$-coordinate is always exactly between the two zeros:

$\text{vertex's }x\text{-coordinate}=\dfrac{\goldD{1}+(\goldD{-7})}{2}=\blueD{-3}$

To find the vertex's $y$-coordinate, we can plug $x=\blueD{-3}$ into $h$:

$\begin{align}
h(-3)&=\dfrac{1}{4}(\blueD{-3} -1)(\blueD{-3} +7)
\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{4}(-4)(4)
\\\\
&=-4
\end{align}$

##Graphing

So the vertex is at $(-3,-4)$.

In order to graph, we need the vertex and another point. That other point can be one of the zeros we found, like $(1,0)$:

[[☃ grapher 2]]

##Finding the zeros

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{5}{9}(x +9)&(x +3)=0
\\\\
\swarrow\quad&\quad\searrow
\\\\
x +9=0\quad&\quad x +3=0
\\\\
x=\goldD{-9}\quad&\quad x=\goldD{-3}
\end{align}$

##Finding the vertex

The vertex's $x$-coordinate is always exactly between the two zeros:

$\text{vertex's }x\text{-coordinate}=\dfrac{\goldD{-9}+(\goldD{-3})}{2}=\blueD{-6}$

To find the vertex's $y$-coordinate, we can plug $x=\blueD{-6}$ into $f$:

$\begin{align}
f(-6)&=\dfrac{5}{9}(\blueD{-6} +9)(\blueD{-6} +3)
\\\\
&=\dfrac{5}{9}(3)(-3)
\\\\
&=-5
\end{align}$

##Graphing

So the vertex is at $(-6,-5)$.

In order to graph, we need the vertex and another point. That other point can be one of the zeros we found, like $(-9,0)$:

[[☃ grapher 2]]

##Finding the zeros

$\begin{align}
-6(x -2)&(x )=0
\\\\
\swarrow\quad&\quad\searrow
\\\\
x -2=0\quad&\quad x=\goldD{0}
\\\\
x=\goldD{2}\quad&\quad 
\end{align}$

##Finding the vertex

The vertex's $x$-coordinate is always exactly between the two zeros:

$\text{vertex's }x\text{-coordinate}=\dfrac{\goldD{2}+\goldD{0}}{2}=\blueD{1}$

To find the vertex's $y$-coordinate, we can plug $x=\blueD{1}$ into $g$:

$\begin{align}
g(1)&=-6(\blueD{1} -2)(\blueD{1} )
\\\\
&=-6(-1)(1)
\\\\
&=6
\end{align}$

##Graphing

So the vertex is at $(1,6)$.

In order to graph, we need the vertex and another point. That other point can be one of the zeros we found, like $(2,0)$:

[[☃ grapher 2]]

##Finding the zeros

$\begin{align}
-4(x -3)&(x -1)=0
\\\\
\swarrow\quad&\quad\searrow
\\\\
x -3=0\quad&\quad x -1=0
\\\\
x=\goldD{3}\quad&\quad x=\goldD{1}
\end{align}$

##Finding the vertex

The vertex's $x$-coordinate is always exactly between the two zeros:

$\text{vertex's }x\text{-coordinate}=\dfrac{\goldD{3}+\goldD{1}}{2}=\blueD{2}$

To find the vertex's $y$-coordinate, we can plug $x=\blueD{2}$ into $h$:

$\begin{align}
h(2)&=-4(\blueD{2} -3)(\blueD{2} -1)
\\\\
&=-4(-1)(1)
\\\\
&=4
\end{align}$

##Graphing

So the vertex is at $(2,4)$.

In order to graph, we need the vertex and another point. That other point can be one of the zeros we found, like $(3,0)$:

[[☃ grapher 2]]

##Finding the zeros

$\begin{align}
-\dfrac{2}{3}(x +1)&(x -5)=0
\\\\
\swarrow\quad&\quad\searrow
\\\\
x +1=0\quad&\quad x -5=0
\\\\
x=\goldD{-1}\quad&\quad x=\goldD{5}
\end{align}$

##Finding the vertex

The vertex's $x$-coordinate is always exactly between the two zeros:

$\text{vertex's }x\text{-coordinate}=\dfrac{\goldD{-1}+\goldD{5}}{2}=\blueD{2}$

To find the vertex's $y$-coordinate, we can plug $x=\blueD{2}$ into $f$:

$\begin{align}
f(2)&=-\dfrac{2}{3}(\blueD{2} +1)(\blueD{2} -5)
\\\\
&=-\dfrac{2}{3}(3)(-3)
\\\\
&=6
\end{align}$

##Graphing

So the vertex is at $(2,6)$.

In order to graph, we need the vertex and another point. That other point can be one of the zeros we found, like $(-1,0)$:

[[☃ grapher 2]]

##Finding the zeros

$\begin{align}
-\dfrac{1}{3}(x +2)&(x +8)=0
\\\\
\swarrow\quad&\quad\searrow
\\\\
x +2=0\quad&\quad x +8=0
\\\\
x=\goldD{-2}\quad&\quad x=\goldD{-8}
\end{align}$

##Finding the vertex

The vertex's $x$-coordinate is always exactly between the two zeros:

$\text{vertex's }x\text{-coordinate}=\dfrac{\goldD{-2}+(\goldD{-8})}{2}=\blueD{-5}$

To find the vertex's $y$-coordinate, we can plug $x=\blueD{-5}$ into $g$:

$\begin{align}
g(-5)&=-\dfrac{1}{3}(\blueD{-5} +2)(\blueD{-5} +8)
\\\\
&=-\dfrac{1}{3}(-3)(3)
\\\\
&=3
\end{align}$

##Graphing

So the vertex is at $(-5,3)$.

In order to graph, we need the vertex and another point. That other point can be one of the zeros we found, like $(-2,0)$:

[[☃ grapher 2]]
